coredata.dc:title: Tuning optical properties of printable carbon quantum dots using near-field environment
coredata.dc:creator: Bhattacharjee, Lopamudra
Pal, Kaushik
Jat, Sanjeev Kumar
Koner, Apurba L.
Ravichandran, Joghee
Bhattacharjee, Rama Ranjan
coredata.dc:description: Tuning emission properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a challenge as limited domain size of sp2 islands in the CQDs restricts electron mobility in one dimension. The result is the formation of often blue and green light-emitting CQDs. The present manuscript depicts the transformation of CQDs into printable ink material and its utilization in fabricating films with tunable emission properties. Excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs were studied in powder form, aqueous suspension, freely suspended polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, printed impressions (PIs) and as thin-films respectively. Analysis of spectroscopic and microscopic data showed a substantial near-field effect in powder, CQD-embedded PVA (PVA-CQD) films and PIs. Light-responsive current-voltage characteristics in PVA-CQD films were explained in the light of interparticle-interaction induced near-field effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot (CQD)
Near-field effect
Printable ink
Screen-printing
Light-responsive film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of spin-coated glass slide for EPI fluorescence microscopy
Photostability studies with printed over-head projector (OHP) sheets
Optical properties of CQD in aqueous suspension
Emission of CQDs in freely-suspended polymer films
Emission properties of screen-printed PSS-CQD ink
Photostability of CQDs in printed impression and PVA film
Tunable emission in PSS-CQDs: near-field effect
Effect of solar-simulator light on I-V characteristics of PVA-CQD films

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: RRB thanks Department of Science & Technology (New Delhi, India) for providing financial support through WTI/2K15/60 ; KP and ALK thank IISER Bhopal for the infrastructural facilities and startup funding. Finally, we are thankful to the editor and anonymous referees for their invaluable suggestions. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of PSS-CQDs showing (a) dispersed particles with average size distribution (5 ± 2) nm and (b) lattice spacing of individual CQDs.
(a) Diffuse reflectance spectrum of PSS-CQDs; (b) Emission spectra of powder PSS-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–Vis spectra of dilute aqueous PSS-CQD suspension; (b) Emission spectra of aqueous PSS-CQD suspension (0.01 wt %) recorded at various excitation wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Fluorescence emission spectra obtained by exciting PSS-CQDs at 290 and 360 nm and their corresponding fluorescence excitation spectra (FES) recorded at 385 and 460 nm respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Concentration-dependent excitation and emission spectra of aqueous PSS-CQDs suspensions. All emission spectra were recorded at 360 nm excitation and all excitation spectra were recorded at 460 nm. Circled portion indicate the occurrence of low energy FES bands. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Excitation-dependent behavior of PVA-CQD film recorded at various excitation wavelengths (320–490 nm) (a) 10 F, (b) 80 F and (c) 200 F; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra obtained by exciting PVA-CQDs (80 F) at 370 and 430 nm and their corresponding excitation spectra (FES) recorded at 470 and 510 nm respectively. The shaded portions between 550 and 600 nm shown in a, b & c highlight the extent of overall red-shift of FL bands with increasing CQD content in the films. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Fluorescence lifetime decay of (a) 10 F and (b) 80 F at varying wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Emission spectrum of the printed impression of PSS-CQDs (λ ex = 340 nm) on OHP sheet and its de-convoluted form. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

EPI-fluorescence studies of printed CQDs on OHP sheet showing (a) original printed impression under normal light and (b, c & d) under UV, blue and green filters respectively. Results of photostability studies under respective filters are shown in e, f & g. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Light-harvesting property of PVA-CQD films. Typical I-V characteristic of PVA-CQD films in dark and under solar simulator (a) 80 F (b) 140 F (c) 200 F; (d) Light response performed on 200 F at 2.5 V (up to three cycles). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Pictorial representation showing the effect of solar simulator light-illumination on the band properties of CQDs embedded in PVA film in (a) absence and (b) presence of near-field effect.
Summary of emission data obtained from Fig. 6 and Fig. S6.

head.ce:title: Tuning optical properties of printable carbon quantum dots using near-field environment

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Coimbatore 641004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Kolkata Campus, West Bengal, 700135, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Nanotechnology, Amity University, Kolkata Campus, West Bengal, 700135, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tuning emission properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a challenge as limited domain size of sp2 islands in the CQDs restricts electron mobility in one dimension. The result is the formation of often blue and green light-emitting CQDs. The present manuscript depicts the transformation of CQDs into printable ink material and its utilization in fabricating films with tunable emission properties. Excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs were studied in powder form, aqueous suspension, freely suspended polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film, printed impressions (PIs) and as thin-films respectively. Analysis of spectroscopic and microscopic data showed a substantial near-field effect in powder, CQD-embedded PVA (PVA-CQD) films and PIs. Light-responsive current-voltage characteristics in PVA-CQD films were explained in the light of interparticle-interaction induced near-field effect.

ce:section.ce:para: The miniaturization of future light-sensitive devices is limited by diffraction effects due to the wave nature of light. Moreover, the most desirable and challenging aspect is scaling down of the typical size of these optical elements within nano-regime. Researchers are currently looking for sustainable solutions in the area of photonic devices. In this regard, noble metal nanoparticles have shown a path towards the development of nanophotonics. The surface plasmons generated in metal nanoparticles have somewhat facilitated the realization of tuning optical properties within nano-domain. Mulvaney and coworkers have experimentally determined scattering spectra of gold nanorod (GNR) dimers arranged in different orientations [1]. They have shown the presence of plasmon hybridization due to near-field coupling of the nanorods. El Sayed and coworkers have shown that gold nanoparticle (GNP) assembly can provide alternative concepts in analyte-sensing applications [2,3]. Shear based orientation of GNRs dispersed in a polymer matrix was shown to create dynamic changes in low energy absorption properties in polymer-GNR composite [4]. Bhattacharjee et al. have reported that chain like self assembly of spherical gold nanoparticles can give rise to distinct absorption profile of GNPs compared to their dilute samples [5]. Thus there exist many such published sciences on the near-field effect with metal nanostructures. Such near-field effects are interesting and possess huge potential in future nano-photonic applications.

The disadvantage of metal-based nanomaterials for futuristic photonic devices may be their production cost and adverse environmental impacts. Thus, it is necessary to identify alternative nanomaterials those are abundant, cheap, scalable and eco-friendly, for near-field studies. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are currently viewed as promising and environmentally benign nanomaterials as they are composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen [6,7]. Investigations on the structure of CQDs reveal the presence of isolated sp2 islands separated or dispersed in sp3 matrix [7–9]. The CQDs can be compared with organic nanoparticles composed of molecular aggregates that emit solid-state fluorescence (FL) [10,11]. In recent years, the emission property of CQDs with suitable surface functionality has been utilized for various applications [12–22]. Emission properties in CQDs originate from the combination of strong quantum confinement and surface defect sites [8,23,24]. Wen et al. have used ultrafast spectroscopy to establish that the FL of CQDs consists of two spectral overlapped bands that can be ascribed to the intrinsic and extrinsic fluorescence [25,26]. The former is attributed to the sp2 nano-domains and the later to the surface states [11].

In order to study near-field effects with CQDs, it is important that these nanomaterials can be assembled in a matrix where their interparticle-interactions can be controlled. This is important to achieve a tunable optical response of the assembled materials. As carbon is a hydrophobic material, it is mandatory that the surface of CQDS should be capped with molecules that offer easy dispersion in solvents like water. Polymer-stabilized CQDs and their optical properties of in suspension have been extensively reported with appreciable quantum yield and biological applications [7,27–29]. The origin of the strong emission in polymer-stabilized CQDs is stable surface-trap sites [30–33]. Literature suggests that these surface trap sites create low-lying energy levels and facilitate effective radiative-recombination [8,34,35]. Polymer-passivated CQDs show appreciable quantum yield and enhanced processibility through control over size, crystallinity and surface sites [8,31]. Several authors have reported FL of polymer-passivated CQDs in suspension [7,31,36–39]. Few reports on FL properties in solid state, polymer films and fabricated structures are available [40,41]. De and coworkers have recently reported that polymer-coated CQDs for the fabrication of transparent fluorescent coatings [42]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on near-field studies of polymer-stabilized CQDs and its effect on the fluorescence and other physical properties of the composite. Moreover, understanding of non-covalent interactions of CQDs with macromolecules like polymer can be an important advancement in the field of tuning the optical properties of CQDs and provide further insight into the origin of optical modulation.

We have recently reported conducting properties of single polymer-passivated CQDs where the presence of polymer on the surface of the CQDs helps to create low lying energy states and facilitate high mobility of electrons [43,44]. W Herein, we report tunable optical properties of polymer-stabilized CQDs in various forms like powder, aqueous suspension, free-standing polymer films and printed impressions (PIs). All the samples were thoroughly characterized and the reason for tunability in emission characteristics was studied. Light responsive properties of CQDs in suspended polymer films were also investigated.

Citric acid (99.7%) was purchased from Himedia Chemicals. Poly (sodium 4-styrene sulfonate, 99.8%, M.W. 70,000) (PSS) and 8-Anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (1,8-ANS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and they were used as received without further purification. Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) (Sigma-Aldrich, M.W. 1,46,000–1,86,000, 98–99% hydrolyzed) was used for preparation of CQD-embedded PVA (PVA-CQD) film. Poly (dimethyl diallyl ammonium) [PDDAC] (Aldrich, M.W. 100 kDa) was used for layer-by-layer self-assembly. Methanol, acetone, hydrogen peroxide, sulphuric acid and ammonia were purchased from Loba Chemie and used without purification. Dialysis membranes were purchased from Himedia. Milli-Q water was used in all experiments. All the measurements were performed at room temperature (25 °C) unless otherwise mentioned.

CQDs were synthesized in a one step pyrolysis process from citric acid where PSS has been taken as a stabilizer and was reported in our previous publications [33]. 3 g of anhydrous CA and 0.5 g of PSS were dissolved in Milli-Q water followed by evaporation at 80 °C until the dry powder was obtained. The powder was kept in a vacuum desiccator for further removal of moisture. The powder was pyrolyzed for 2 h at 200 °C in a tubular furnace. Finally, the brownish-black powder was obtained which was used in experiments. Purification of PSS-passivated CQDs (PSS-CQDs) was done by dialyzing aqueous suspension by snake-skin dialysis membrane.

Polymer solution (3 wt %) was prepared with PVA by slowly dissolving 3 gm of polymer in 100 ml of water. Solution was kept under stirring for one day at 70 °C. A clear PVA solution was obtained. PVA-CQD films were prepared with different CQD loading ranging from 10 to 200 mg of powder CQDs. 10 ml of purified powder CQDs (10 mg) aqueous suspension was dissolved in 10 ml of PVA solution and stirred overnight. Later, the solution was poured in a Petri dish with uniform thickness and diameter. The Petri dish was kept on water bath to evaporate out excess water followed by drying in vacuum oven. A free standing PVA-CQD film was obtained. Similarly, few more PVA-CQD films were made with 30 mg, 50 mg, 80 mg, 140 mg and 200 mg CQDs respectively. The films were named as 10 F, 30 F, 50 F, 80 F, 140 F and 200 F respectively depending on CQD loading and the same has been mentioned in the present manuscript. Optical studies of the film were performed using spectrofluorometer and UV–Vis spectrometer using solid-state accessories. A piece of PVA-CQD film (1 cm × 1 cm) was clamped between two IC connectors and current was recorded at a voltage regime of −3 V to 3 V. Thicknesses of the films was 0.56 mm. I-V data were recorded with Keithley 2450 source meter unit. Solar simulator from ABET Technologies was used for illuminating the samples to study light harvesting property of PVA-CQD samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of spin-coated glass slide for EPI fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Glass slides were washed with soap water and then sonicated several times with double distilled water. Similar procedure was adopted for washing with acetone. After that the slides were treated with a mixture of water, ammonia and hydrogen peroxide in a ratio of 5:3:1 for 30 min followed by washing with Milli-Q water. Slides were dried purging nitrogen gas. Before spin coating, slides were kept under UV (long wavelength) light for 1 h. After UV exposure, the glass slides were spin coated with 0.1 wt % aqueous CQD suspensions at 1500 rpm for 100 s by Laurel spin coater. Spin coated glass slides were characterized by AFM and EPI fluorescence.

Steady state absorption measurements were done with Shimadzu UV-spectrophotometer using 10 mm path length quartz cuvettes. All the steady state measurements in solutions were carried out using HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorimax-4 fluorimeter. An aqueous suspension of CQDs (0.01 wt %) was taken for all the measurements to keep the absorption value minimum as to avoid inner filter effect. The experiments were carried out at pH 2.0, 4.0, 7.0 and 9.0, where pH was controlled using HCl and NaOH solutions. All the CQD stock solutions were prepared depending on their water-dispersion abilities. Fluorescence spectra were recorded by 10 mm path length quartz cuvette in the region from 355 nm to 680 nm range by exciting at 340 nm with both excitation and emission slits operated with a width of 2 nm. Fluorescence spectra of printed CQDs were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301PC Spectrofluorometer fitted with both solution and solid sample accessories. All the experiments were carried out at room temperature. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were performed using a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) setup consists of an Ortec 9327 pico-timing amplifier and using pulse Diode laser (λ ex = 370 nm) with fwhm ∼175 ps with a setup target 10,000 counts. The instrument response function was measured before and after fluorescence lifetime measurement using a dilute Ludox solution (purchased from Sigma). The emission polarizer was positioned at magic angle (54.7°) polarization with respect to excitation polarizer. Fitting function was employed by iterative deconvolution method using supplied software DAS v6.2. The quality of the fitted data was judged from the reduced chi-squared value (χ 2), calculated using the IBH software provided with the instrument. All the measurements were carried out at ambient temperature (298 K).

Grid preparation: 0.1 mg mL−1 aqueous suspension of brownish CQDs were prepared and kept for 24 h dialysis. 10 μL of dialyzed CQDs aqueous suspension were drop casted on carbon-coated copper grid having 300 mesh structures for TEM analysis.

HR-TEM images were taken in a JEOL JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope at operating voltage of 200 kV with LaB6 filament. Elemental analysis was carried out during HR-TEM analysis using Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy recorded by Oxford Instruments. Lattice d-spacing values of individual CQDs were calculated using FFT method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability studies with printed over-head projector (OHP) sheets
ce:section.ce:para: Concentrated aqueous slurry (200 mg ml −1) of the CQDs was used as ink and printed on OHP sheet using an ordinary screen printer. A piece of printed OHP sheet was exposed to solar simulator light. Every 30 min interval, EPI-fluorescence images were recorded and total exposure time was 5 h. Fluorescence images of printed impressions were obtained using UV (Excitation wavelength 330–380 nm), blue (Excitation wavelength 450–490 nm) and green (Excitation wavelength 515–560 nm) filters in inverted phase contrast fluorescence Nikon eclipses TI series EPI fluorescence microscope. Photostability study was done by solar simulator from ABET Technologies.

The main objective of the study is to find out how the FL properties of CQDs can be tuned by inducing near-field environment. Hence, it is necessary to synthesize well defined CQDs with pronounced FL properties and high quantum yield (QY). The CQDs also need to be highly dispersible in a given solvent so that it can be easily fabricated. This is required to tune its environment by dispersing it in different media.

Citric acid (CA) and Poly (sodium 4-styrene sulfonate, PSS) were used to produce CQDs through a one-step modified-citric acid pyrolysis process [43,45]. Here CA served as the carbon source, while PSS was used to provide surface passivation and control over the size of CQDs. High QY values (40–48% with respect to 8-Anilino-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid (1,8 ANS in methanol as standard) were obtained from the PSS-CQDs that may be attributed to the presence of PSS on CQD surface. Average QY was obtained from triplicate measurements. Similar high values of QY are also known for polymer-passivated CQDs [8]. Presence of PSS on CQD surface makes PSS-CQD powder easily dispersible in aqueous suspension. Role of PSS as passivating agent has been explained in details in our recent work and was further verified by pyrolyzing CA in absence of PSS [43]. The black powder obtained from the pyrolysis of pure CA was completely insoluble in water. This shows that only attachment of PSS molecules on CQD surface renders it hydrophilic and highly dispersible in water.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was used to study the size distribution and lattice properties of PSS-CQDs. Fig. 1 a and b shows PSS-CQDs with average size distribution (5 ± 2 nm) and their crystalline fringes respectively. In presence of strong interatomic-interactions, amorphous solids like carbon do not typically show long-range order [46]. Medium range order may be due to formation of isolated sp2 islands present in the PSS-CQDs [8]. Fig. 1b shows lattice d-spacing value averaging 0.3 nm resembles graphitic lattice [8,47]. The presence of lattice ordering and possible stabilization of surface defects states due to polymer-passivation may be responsible for high QY values obtained for the PSS-CQDs. Purity and functionalization of the PSS-CQDs was confirmed by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) performed during HR-TEM studies and FT-IR spectroscopy as provided in Supplementary data (Fig. S1). EDX data shows 93% by weight carbon present in CQDs compared to 81% for blank TEM-grid (Fig. S1b). FT-IR data of both pure PSS and P-CQDs are shown for comparison (Fig. S1c). The data shows the presence of PSS with the CQDs along with carbonyl functionality (∼1700 cm −1) which is well reported for CQDs [43]. The above experimental outcomes regarding high QY, appreciable water dispersibility and defined structure of the synthesized CQDs enable us to use the PSS-CQDs for near-field studies.

Optical properties of PSS-CQDs were studied in pure form and as polymer composites. FL of Powder CQDs were studied using diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and fluorescence spectroscopy with solid-state accessories. DRS spectrum of powder sample indicates CQDs absorb well into the visible spectrum with prominent bands at 225, 300 and 400 nm (Fig. 2 a). The bands at 300 and 400 nm do not correspond to that of the precursor materials as evidenced from the DRS spectrum of the precursors (Fig. S2). Emission spectra of powder PSS-CQDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths (370–560 nm) are shown in Fig. 2b. The intensity of fluorescence emission (FL) band is very weak below 370 nm excitation but gradually intensifies as the excitation energy is decreased. The nature of the FL band is broad and covers almost the whole visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum. The broadness of the FL band might be attributed to interparticle-interaction as reported for semiconductor quantum dots [48]. There is the appearance of a single broad band located around 570 nm (maxima) upon excitation at 450 nm (Fig. 2b). Beyond 450 nm excitation, the broad FL band develops into two overlapping bands with the maximum emission red-shifted towards 550–650 nm. The band at 630 nm gradually sharper and intensifies upon lower energy excitation (beyond 520 nm) without any signs of FL quenching of PSS-CQDs in powder form. The Raman bands that appear in the FL spectra indicated in Fig. 2b gradually overlaps with the emission band at ∼600 nm. The optical properties were further studied in concentrated suspensions and thin-films as shown in following sections.

The FL data obtained from powder samples shows that the FL emission of PSS-CQDs in solid state covers almost the whole visible region of electromagnetic spectrum and may further extend into the near-IR region. Such interesting trends are promising for tunability in FL emissions due to near-field environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CQD in aqueous suspension
ce:section.ce:para: Powder PSS-CQDs are highly dispersible as aqueous colloidal suspension due to the presence of PSS on the surface [33]. Known concentrations of PSS-CQDs are dispersed in water. Absorption properties of PSS-CQDs aqueous suspensions were studied using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 3 a). UV–Vis spectrum of dilute aqueous PSS-CQD suspension (0.005 wt %) shows a prominent band ∼225 nm and a broad band ∼350 nm (Fig. 3a). The band at 350 nm may correspond to the band at 300 nm present in powder CQDs. The red-shift of ∼50 nm might be due to the interaction of CQD with solvents and stabilization of the surface states. The band at 225 nm observed both in powder and in suspension can be attributed to the π-bonding present in sp2 islands present in the PSS-CQDs [8].

The experimental findings from optical studies of PSS-CQDs in solid and in suspension indicate that their FL properties are dependent on the local environment of the CQDs. Solvent and quenching effects may also alter the FL properties. In order to avoid the quenching effects due to collision and solvent properties, we choose to study the emission properties of PSS-CQDs in polymer matrix and other thin-films where the quenching effects due to interparticle collision and Brownian motion may be minimized to a large extent. It will also be feasible to study emission properties at higher PSS-CQD concentrations in such films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission of CQDs in freely-suspended polymer films
ce:section.ce:para: Presence of PSS on CQD surface provides suitable miscibility with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) in aqueous suspension. Freely suspended uniform films of PVA-CQD were obtained after casting and drying process. EPI-fluorescence images and time dependent fluorescence intensity data of the films shown in supplementary data confirms the uniformity distribution of fluorescent CQDs in the films (Fig. S4). The films were transparent and their transparency was recorded (Fig. S5). Fig. 6 a shows emission spectra of PVA-CQD film (10 F) obtained at various excitation wavelengths (320–490 nm). The data shows excitation-independent behavior of PVA-CQD films in the excitation regime of 320–370 nm. Beyond that, the film shows excitation-dependent emission spectra (Fig. 6a). The emission spectra of higher loadings of CQD (80 F & 200 F) are shown in Fig. 6 b & c. The data shows that with increase in PSS-CQDs, B2 band shifts towards near IR region (550–600 nm). For clarity, the red-shift has been indicated through the shaded region as highlighted in Fig. 6(a–c). The emission data collected from all the spectra were summarized Table 1 . Emission data of samples 40 F and 140 F are provided in Fig. S6. The gradual red-shift in emission of the PVA-CQD films may be attributed to near-field environment induced by the increase in interparticle-interactions between CQDs in the PVA film. The same was supported by excitation-emission spectra of 80 F as shown in Fig. 6d and lifetime studies (Fig. 7 ).

FES data of 80 F is shown in Fig. 6d. The FES band was not mirror image of the corresponding B2 bands and showed more than one maxima located at ∼380 nm and ∼410 nm. The band at 380 nm corresponds to the 360 nm FES maxima observed in aqueous PSS-CQDs (Fig. 4). The 20 nm red-shift may be due to change in dielectric environment of the dispersion medium. The low energy FES band at ∼410 nm may have appeared due to near-field induced by interparticle-interactions. The electron cloud of individual CQDs may overlap with neighboring CQDs inside the PVA matrix and create low-lying energy levels similar to that of hybridized GNRs [1]. FES corresponding to 510 nm emission shows further red shift of the ∼410 band to ∼ 425 nm (Fig. 6d). The above trend in shift of emission maxima with change in concentration of PSS-CQDs in a given medium (PVA matrix) shows the effect of near-field environment on the tunability in emission properties of PSS-CQDs.

Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopic measurements of PVA-CQD films were performed for further investigation of the near-field effects and better correlation with steady-state spectroscopy. All lifetime decay curves are fitted with three exponential decay kinetics (Fig. 7). These multi-exponential lifetimes of the fluorescence imply the presence of various emissive states, which is due to a wide range of chemical environments and interdot interactions. To mention, that the bare CQD with similar size distribution has 2.4 ns fluorescence lifetime in neutral water under dilute condition (data not shown). As the photophysical properties of CQD are highly dependent on the nature of its surface. Therefore, we anticipated an interaction of polymer matrix with CQD surface and consequently, a change in the fluorescence lifetime properties. As expected the excited-state lifetime increases from 2.4 ns to 2.8 ns when monitored as 405 nm. The fluorescence lifetime increased gradually as moving from lower to higher monitoring wavelength. In case of PVA -CQDs, we have measured lifetime decays at every 25 nm ranging from 405 to 580 nm. Fluorescence lifetime of CQDs gets enhanced from (2.8 ± 0.2) ns to (5.0 ± 0.2) ns when lifetime decay was measured at 405 nm and 580 nm respectively. Further, we have also varied the loading percentage of CQDs in the PVA polymer matrix and in all case a similar trend in increase in the luminescence lifetime is observed. A representative figure of wavelength-dependent emission lifetime decay plot of F10 and F80 are shown in Fig. 7 a–b. Non-mono exponential decay in case of CQD and PVA -CQDs is due to the presence of different emitters e.g., exciton dynamics, photo-induced changes on the surface, interparticle interactions etc. The excitation wavelength dependent increase in the fluorescence lifetime as measured from each emission decay may be explained by the presence of different decay pathways and their corresponding rates of electron-hole recombination from the core and surface states generated from photo excitation [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emission properties of screen-printed PSS-CQD ink
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study near-field effects in concentrated aqueous suspensions, the PSS-CQDs were fabricated on overhead projector (OHP) sheet using screen-printing. Concentrated aqueous suspension (200 mg mL−1) of the CQDs was used as ink to generate stable printed impressions (PIs) (Figs. S7 and S8). Fig. S8 shows the EPI-fluorescence image of the letter ‘A’ under three different filters. Emission spectrum (excitation at 340 nm) obtained from PI shows a broad band centered at 530 nm extending well into the red region up to 650 nm (Fig. 8 ). The broad spectra indicate the presence of strong near-field effect compared to PVA-CQD films. The appearance of RGB colors observed in PI (Figs. S9 and 9) can be explained from the broad emission spectrum (Fig. 8). De-convoluted form of the emission curve shows the presence of three overlapping bands of red, green and blue emission (RGB) and hence explains the occurrence of the RGB colors observed in PI. The presence of PSS on CQD surface provides stability to the PIs as the PSS-CQDs adhere efficiently to the OHP sheet surface without quenching. The above findings are contrary to the findings reported in literature where FL of CQDs were quenched upon printing on plastic material [41]. Hence the optical data obtained from the studies with PSS-CQDs printed over flexible surface offers a method to study near-field effects in concentrated aqueous suspensions of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of CQDs in printed impression and PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: The use of printable CQDs for near-field studies is the most important aspect of the current manuscript. It not only provides the opportunity to study emission properties at higher concentrations, but also offer easier technique to fabricate the material. Further, phtostability studies were performed with PIs to understand the long-term stability of the emission properties. EPI-fluorescence microscope imaging after exposure to solar simulator showed three distinct fluorescent colors in the PI under UV, blue and green filters (Fig. 9 ). Images were recorded from edges of the PI, which enable one to visualize the blank (due to OHP sheet) and printed portions indicated in Fig. 9b, c & d. The dark blank portions show absence of background stray light that indicates light in PI is solely due to FL. The relative intensity of each RGB color was obtained using average intensities from 100 different points on the PI surface using fluorescence microscope imaging software. Intensity data plotted with time (Fig. 9e, f & g) were collected with respect to the dark region (blank OHP Sheet) for each color separately. Results obtained from the plot of the fluorescence intensity over time (5 h) showed overall photostability of the three fluorescent RGB colors. Interestingly, we observed that the blue and green light intensities did not deviate with the exception of the red color. The deviations in red emission can be attributed to mixed-sized CQDs present in the sample [18]. There was no change in FL of the PI for more than one week in the air and at ambient temperature (Fig. S9). PVA-CQD films also showed uniform photostable features as shown in supplementary data (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tunable emission in PSS-CQDs: near-field effect
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental results obtained from the studies of PSS-CQDs in various environments indicate that optical properties of CQDs are not solely governed by the strong emission generated from surface trap states. Interparticle-interaction and extended sp2 conjugation among closely spaced CQDs can result in tunable emission by the creation of low-lying energy levels. Concentration dependent emission properties of an aqueous suspension of PSS-CQDs showed initial indications of near-field effect (Fig. 5). FES data at concentrations beyond 0.05% showed low energy band in addition to the original FES band as shown by the circle in Fig. 5. Though the low-lying energy bands were detected at higher CQD concentrations, quenching effects also prevailed. Thus, FES data was not sufficiently convincing to justify the near-field effect. It was necessary to fabricate the CQDs in a polymer matrix where higher CQD loadings can be achieved and quenching effect due to interparticle-collisions can be avoided. FES data recorded for CQDs in PVA matrix showed an appearance of prominent low energy FES band with increased CQD loading (Fig. 6d). Such observations confirm the theory of near-field effect. Similar interparticle-interaction induced near-field effects have been reported for GNRs and GNPs [1,3,5]. Data obtained from lifetime studies of PVA-CQD films further justified the near-field effect. A gradual increase in the fluorescence lifetime with increasing the monitoring wavelength at higher CQD concentration suggests that the low-lying energy states originated from interparticle interactions (Fig. 7). The decay rates were relatively slower compared to non-interacting CQDs. Similar observations have been reported for aggregated dye molecules in solid state [10]. Finally, highly concentrated CQD suspensions were used as ink and printed on OHP sheets. The PIs showed excellent near-field effect by displaying RGB color in the emission spectrum. Controlled assemblies of well-spaced CQDs obtained from fabrication technique like spin-coating did not show any sign of red-shift in emission of PSS-CQDs that confirms the near-field effect in closely-spaced CQDs (Fig. S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solar-simulator light on I-V characteristics of PVA-CQD films
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs may allow effective absorption of visible solar light as indicated in the DRS spectrum of powder sample (Fig. 2a). The same was observed when different compositions of PVA-CQDs were exposed to solar simulator light and the current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of the films were recorded under illumination. When illuminated, the current increased ∼10 folds as shown in Fig. 10 . In order to carry out I-V measurements, a piece of PVA-CQD film was clamped between two IC connectors and current was recorded at a voltage regime of −3 V to 3 V. Fig. 10 (a, b, c) shows the I-V curves of PVA-CQD samples 80 F, 140 F and 200 F respectively. In the absence of solar simulator light (i.e., in dark), a very low current (I) value was recorded (0.01 nA) may be due to insulating nature of PVA matrix surrounding CQDs. It was observed that the value of current (I) increased when the 200 F and 140 F films were exposed to solar simulator. Removal of light showed decrease of current as shown in Fig. 10d. In case of lower concentration of CQDs in the film (80 F & below), change in current upon exposure to solar simulator light was negligible. Controlled experiments with pure PVA film and PVA mixed with precursors did not produce any change upon light illumination.

The observations on the effect of light on PVA-CQD films may be explained via a schematic mechanism (Scheme 1 ). The scheme shows the change in band diagram in presence and absence of near-field effects in CQDs. It also shows the charge transport towards the electrode through polymer matrix during light actuation. Scheme 1 (part a) shows the effect of light on the band properties of sample 80 F. At low concentration of CQDs in PVA (80 F & below), near-field effect due to interparticle-interactions is less pronounced. Hence, charge transport through electron movement gets hindered due to the insulating nature of PVA matrix in both directions. Above that, the trap state energy levels are not sufficiently stabilized as near-field effect is weak in these samples. Thus the effective band gap as shown in part a of Scheme 1 is appreciable which shows negligible photoconductivity in 80 F (Fig. 10a). The higher electrical conductivity observed under light for samples 140 F and 200 F may be due to the occurrence of low lying energy bands corresponding to surface trap states (STS) as reflected in FES data of the PVA-CQD films (Fig. 6d). Literature suggests that, with the minimization of interparticle-distance between nanoparticles, there is considerable red-shift in absorption properties [8]. This gives rise to the lowering of band gap and formation of more continuous stationary states. In case of PSS-CQDS, with an increase in number of particles in the PVA matrix, the near-field effect becomes stronger along with the overlap between discrete sp2 islands present in the CQDs. There is substantial lowering in STS as indicated from emission data. The overall effect is increased delocalization of surface electrons over the large surface area. Such effect can effectively create near-field effect which facilitates mobility of electrons under light actuation as shown in part b of Scheme 1. The lowering of STS may enhance the photoconductivity as observed in Fig. 10b and c. Thus near-field effect manifested by red-shifted emission properties was shown to enhance light harvesting properties in PVA-CQD films.

In summary, we have shown that polymer-passivated, water dispersible and printable CQDs display tunable emission characteristics using near-field environment. The tunable emission has been attributed to concentration dependent near-field effects and inter-dot couplings present in concentrated suspensions and fabricated structures of PSS-CQDs. The near-field effects may have played an important role in stabilizing surface trap states within CQD assemblies. It was shown that small changes in local CQD assembly could create relatively large change in the overall optical properties of the QDs in the assembly or the resulting composite. Fluorescence emission originated from printed CQDs showed RGB light. The same was stable with respect to photo-thermal treatments. Near-field induced photoconductivity under visible illumination was observed for PVA-CQD films. We believe that the findings reported in this manuscript may provide a better perspective for designing printable CQDs for nano-photonic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: RRB thanks Department of Science & Technology (New Delhi, India) for providing financial support through WTI/2K15/60; KP and ALK thank IISER Bhopal for the infrastructural facilities and startup funding. Finally, we are thankful to the editor and anonymous referees for their invaluable suggestions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: EDS analysis of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), FT-IR data, DRS of pure PSS-citric acid mixture, concentration-dependent emission spectra of PSS-CQDs, EPI-fluorescence images of PVA-CQD film, transmittance spectra of PVA-CQD film, excitation-dependent behavior of PVA-CQD film, printed impression (PI) of CQDs on flexible plastic material and time-dependent EPI-fluorescence microscope image of PI, 2-D & 3-D AFM images of spin-coated CQDs.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.09.030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecular Devices and Machines– a Journey into the Nano World
Electronic and Optical Properties of Closed Packed Cadmium Selenium Quantum Dots Solids
